title: Same Industry, Different Multiples: What Separates a 3x Business from a 6x One
linkedin:
Two businesses in the same sector. Near-identical revenue. One sells for 3x. The other sells for 6x.

The difference is not the industry. It is the structure.

Most owners assume the multiple is handed to them by the market. Trades, three to five times. Professional services, four to seven. The sector sets the band.

What experienced buyers actually do is different. They assess the structural quality of the specific business in front of them. The gap between bottom and top of any sector band consistently traces to three factors.

Owner dependence is the largest. A business where the owner holds the primary client relationships or central technical expertise presents a clear risk: earnings may not fully survive the transition. The practical discount runs to one to two times annual normalised profit. A business at $600,000 in EBITDA might attract 3.5 times under high dependence and 5.5 times once it is substantially reduced. Same profitability. Over $1 million difference.

Revenue predictability is the second factor. Buyers pay more for certainty. Contracted or retainer-based revenue tells a buyer the earnings they are purchasing are durable.

Financial quality is the third. Clean accounts with clear cost categorisation and disciplined receivables reduce buyer uncertainty. Accounts that generate questions at due diligence generate doubt, and doubt erodes both the multiple and deal certainty.

Improvements need three financial years to become credible. EOFY is the natural starting point.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance

Two businesses. Same sector. Same revenue. One sells for 3x, the other for 6x.

The difference isn't the industry. It's structure: how dependent the business is on its owner, how predictable the revenue is, and how clean the financials look to a buyer.

High owner dependence alone typically discounts a valuation by one to two times annual profit. That's a material sum for a business of any size.

If you've been thinking about your business's value, what's the specific factor you think would move your multiple the most?

google:
If you've looked into what businesses like yours sell for, you've probably found a range rather than a number.

A trades business might sell for three to five times normalised profit. A professional services firm, four to seven times. What the range doesn't tell you is where within it your specific business sits.

The gap between the bottom and top of any sector band comes down to structural quality. Three factors that buyers assess consistently: how dependent the business is on the owner's personal relationships and expertise, how predictable and repeatable the revenue is, and how clean and well-managed the financial records are.

High owner dependence alone carries a practical discount of one to two times annual normalised profit. A business generating $600,000 in EBITDA might attract 3.5 times under high owner dependence and 5.5 times once that dependence is reduced. That's over $1 million in enterprise value from a structural change entirely within an owner's control.

The improvements that move a multiple need three financial years to become credible to a buyer's due diligence process. Post-EOFY is the natural moment to understand where the current multiple sits and which structural factors have the highest expected return on effort.

ProfitPulse has published a practical guide to this for East Coast SME owners:
https://profit-pulse.com.au/same-industry-different-multiples-what-separates-a-3x-business-from-a-6x-one
